question: Explain the role of the coefficient matrix A in formulating a system of linear equations for a least-squares regression problem. Why is the method of least squares necessary when the system Ax = b is inconsistent?
expectedPoints: The matrix A is constructed from the data points (e.g., a column of 1s for the intercept and a column of the independent variable values).
The system Ax = b becomes inconsistent when there are more equations (data points) than unknowns (model parameters), meaning no exact solution exists.
Least squares finds the vector x that minimizes the squared Euclidean norm of the residual vector, ||b - Ax||², providing the best-fit solution.

question: Describe the geometric interpretation of the least-squares solution. Specifically, explain the relationship between the vector b, the column space of A (Col A), and the residual vector r = b - Aẋ.
expectedPoints: The least-squares solution ẋ minimizes the distance from b to the column space of A.
The vector Aẋ is the projection of b onto Col A. This is the point in the column space closest to b.
The residual vector r = b - Aẋ is orthogonal to the entire column space of A (i.e., orthogonal to every column of A). This orthogonality is the foundation for deriving the normal equations.

question: Define the normal equations for a least-squares problem. Under what condition does the matrix AᵀA guarantee a unique least-squares solution, and why?
expectedPoints: The normal equations are given by (AᵀA)ẋ = Aᵀb.
A unique least-squares solution exists if and only if the matrix A has linearly independent columns.
If the columns of A are linearly independent, then AᵀA is an invertible matrix. This invertibility guarantees a unique solution for ẋ, calculated as ẋ = (AᵀA)⁻¹Aᵀb.
